author: @jimsnipes1203
text: Here's my idea: take 2 IBCs, one on top of the other, the top one upside down, oh, remove the bladders first. Make some vanes like the Ventum but upside down. make them out of 16 gauge sheet metal and put 4 sided (not circular) inside the IBC frames and use your cone on a truck alternator. There are 5 spaces in each IBC and you should be able to have 7 or 8 vanes. The bottom of the top IBC (it's upside down) to keep direct rain out. The vanes would be curved and held that way by the adjoining vanes, then would be fastened to the rails on the IBC with screws. Of course, the 2 IBCs would be bolted together. Then I would use 4 "T" stakes one on each corner and wire the bottom IBC to the "T" stakes driven into the ground to keep the wind from blowing it over. I live on a ridge and the prevailing wind is at 90 degrees to it. If installed on a roof you would need to fasten it down a different way.

replies.author: @Midknight129
replies.text: I recall seeing videos crop up recently about spinning-cylinder shaped props for drones, but they function more like paddle-wheels.  They also seem to function better when used in conjunction as hybrid-lift models where different types of lifters can be leaned on depending on the needs of the situation. text: I just viewed the tinkercad drawings and I was correct. When using this turbine as a traditional vawt the blades are used in the drag mode because the wind enters into the turbine from the sides and the blades have all their trailing edges pointed outwards. When used as a hawt or a vortex turbine then the blades are used in the lift mode because the fluid enters the turbine from the inside and goes out, utilizing the leading edges of the airfoils for lift mode.

text: This turbine could adapted to a duel use wind and rising hot air generator (or "Atmospheric Vortex Engine"). Vortex generators use the phenomenon of hot air rising to generate power. If you could use solar power to create rising hot air, it could be directed through the turbine to create power when the wind was not blowing. I have some quick thoughts on some changes to make it work, like a double cone on the blade assembly, the bottom one directing the rising air through the blades. Perhaps a "fairing" design that directs both wind and rising hot air through the turbine in the same direction. Lots of things to work out.

author: @Ewr42
text:
"if it's open source it'll get stolen!"

-someone that doesn't know a single thing about the open source movement.

For something to be stolen, first you must legitimize the concept of ownership over it(and good luck on getting around the contradiction of privatizing the common good, which is inherently theft from the whole universe in benefit of that one selfish person that feels entitled to ownership).then you must legitimize the personal ownership yourself, then it's bureaucratically yours.

Then it could be stolen.

But sharing ideas as freely as we receive and transform them is simply acknowledging that science isn't an individual effort, but a collective one.

People must believe in property though, after all, if they work hard enough they can become rich enough to own some, right?(right?)

Love you for genuinely caring about knowledge and creativity instead of a profit motive.

I dream of a world where every scientist is as excited as you about their research, where they're free to choose what to research, where they don't "have" to work(to pay bills and afford food and shelter, which simply shouldn't be stuff you need money to have), and where science isn't a product we produce and then sell at a negative price to a publisher that also bills the readers that subscribe to it..

It's really riveting to see your creativity and passion, Rob!

replies.author: @whatelseison8970
replies.text:
@alexwild4350 Yeah, that's the other thing -- all permanent magnets have something called the Curie Point where they transition from ferromagnetic to paramagnetic, which means they lose their magnetization.  Once they cool back down they can theoretically be remagnetized, but for NdFeB magnets it takes a special type of field coil called a Helmholtz coil to supply a strong field that's also highly uniform. I'd actually like to try it one day just out of curiosity.. 

Anyways, how about if you put your hot exhaust through a turbocharger and use the boost pressure to force in even more air.  Then you can just spin a generator off the turbo shaft.  Then you'd go from a rocket stove to a jet stove co-gen!  Colin Furze has actually done it. Minus the generator bit.

replies.author: @solarsynapse
replies.text: @alexwild4350 Magnets need to stay below 140F, less is even better.  Learned that from RC vehicle motors. High heat can also cause grease dropout of bearings.

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text: Probably not double, because you'd be increasing the mass, so it would need a slightly higher wind-speed to turn and would turn more slowly for a given wind-speed. It would definitely be a higher output though; more voltage if wired in series, more power if wired in parallel. There are also of course greater costs and complexity in the build to consider, So the question becomes; which is better between doing what you suggest or just having 2 of this current design? Depends on your circumstances I guess (like how much room have you got?)

author: @Johnson_Rice
text: I wonder if this cone & finned ring design is more efficient than if you just put a helical propeller (like a pointy whirlybird) inside of the darwin wind  pipe would be....  My thinking is that then ALL the energy of the wind initially redirected by the darwin wind pipe would be directed into the fan blades rather than losing some by being redirected again by the cone... BUT... I'm such a layperson I have no idea... might as well be a child expressing inane thoughts - but I'll throw in anyway in case my musings have merit.  Also, separately, I wonder if it would be more efficient if the cone in the center spun with the finned ring, like an jet engine impeller... which lastly makes me wonder if a duct shaped like a jet impeller duct at the top of the darwin turbine would increase output better than simply have a flat enclosed top... or if having it open at all allows too much pressure to escape....  In wondering these things... I found that there was actually a group designing something like this called the Invelox by Sheerwind that might have some useful ideas to be applied here.

author: @screen-protector
text:
Thank you, Robert and guys in general for this one. I didn't build it yet. Watching the series how it reveal more and more ;). I'd love to see it as maglev solution in the pipe so that the flow the turn several of them one by one ;). Yes, the next one would turn slower, but, still efficient. And the more there is, the slower it could go. The question would be how many of them would make sense in what conditions.

One more thing, I'm checking wind speeds here in Scotland Edinburgh to Glasgow area and we quite often have 6mph, not 3, 4. Not mentioning more rain LOL. So, this have even more sense here though. I'll be happy to build it and already working on a pure sine inverter and a battery charging circuit. Waiting on PCBs and components atm. I plan to do a whole series of episodes on those inverters and battery chargers.

replies.author: @Struthio_Camelus
replies.text: @yaka2490 ... Maybe balance it on the tip of the cone, for a rough balance, first to see if there's a consistent lean.

replies.author: @yaka2490
replies.text: @Struthio_Camelus i have done balancing resin printed high speed tesla turbines already and a proven method... he could stick a small cup of water on the cone and spin up the rotorset and watch the ripples on the water surface... now just use a small wheel on the dremel on the outer rim of the rotor top then bottom so when it vibrates it touches the grind wheel... you will see an imediate improvement as the ripples on the water surface with be vastly reduced.
